annotations.label: Statistical hypothesis test

anchor.snippet: A statistical hypothesis test is a method of statistical inference

annotations.note: Mathlib has a statistical decision-theory framework (estimators as kernels, risk/Bayes risk) but no definition of a hypothesis test itself.

annotations.label: Hypothesis

anchor.snippet: any statement about the parameters describing a population can be a hypothesis

annotations.note: No notion of a statistical hypothesis about population parameters exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Null and alternative hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The test compares two hypotheses

annotations.note: Null/alternative hypotheses are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: p-value and significance level

anchor.snippet: If this probability (called the p-value) is less than the significance level of the test

annotations.note: No concept of p-value or significance level exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Type I error

anchor.snippet: A type I error , in which the null hypothesis is rejected despite the null hypothesis being true

annotations.note: Type I error is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Type II error

anchor.snippet: A type II error , in which the null hypothesis is accepted despite the alternative hypothesis being true

annotations.note: Type II error is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power of the test

anchor.snippet: is called the power of the test

annotations.note: The power of a test is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple hypothesis

anchor.snippet: Simple hypothesis : Any hypothesis which specifies the population distribution completely

annotations.note: No notion of simple hypothesis exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Composite hypothesis

anchor.snippet: Composite hypothesis: Any hypothesis which does not specify the population distribution completely

annotations.note: No notion of composite hypothesis exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Positive data

anchor.snippet: Positive data: Data that enable the investigator to reject a null hypothesis

annotations.note: No such hypothesis-testing data concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Critical values

anchor.snippet: Critical value s of a statistical test are the boundaries of the acceptance region of the test

annotations.note: Critical values of a test are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Acceptance region

anchor.snippet: The acceptance region is the set of values of the test statistic for which the null hypothesis is not rejected

annotations.note: No acceptance region concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Region of rejection / Critical region

anchor.snippet: Region of rejection / Critical region : The set of values of the test statistic for which the null hypothesis is rejected

annotations.note: No critical/rejection region concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Size

anchor.snippet: Size : For simple hypotheses, this is the test's probability of incorrectly rejecting the null hypothesis

annotations.note: The size of a test is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statistical significance test

anchor.snippet: Statistical significance test : A predecessor to the statistical hypothesis test

annotations.note: Significance testing is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conservative test

anchor.snippet: Conservative test: A test is conservative if, when constructed for a given nominal significance level

annotations.note: No notion of a conservative test exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Most powerful test

anchor.snippet: Most powerful test: For a given size or significance level

annotations.note: The most powerful test is not formalized; only general decision-theoretic optimality (minimax/Bayes risk) exists.

annotations.label: Steps of a frequentist hypothesis test

anchor.section: Performing a frequentist hypothesis test in practice
anchor.snippet: The typical steps involved in performing a frequentist hypothesis test in practice are

annotations.note: The procedure of a frequentist hypothesis test is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neyman-Pearson decision rule

anchor.section: Performing a frequentist hypothesis test in practice
anchor.snippet: The Neyman-Pearson decision rule is to reject the null hypothesis

annotations.note: The likelihood ratio underlying the rule is defined (`llr`/`rnDeriv`), but the decision rule itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Radioactive suitcase (Geiger counter)

anchor.snippet: applied to the radioactive suitcase example

annotations.note: This illustrative example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: p-value bounded by significance level

anchor.snippet: When the null hypothesis is true and statistical assumptions are met, the probability that the p-value will be less than or equal to the significance level

annotations.note: The uniform-distribution property of the p-value under the null is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: p-value

anchor.snippet: The p -value is the probability that a test statistic which is at least as extreme as the one obtained would occur under the null hypothesis

annotations.note: The p-value is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rejection rule via p-value

anchor.snippet: then we say the null hypothesis is rejected at the chosen level of significance

annotations.note: The p-value rejection rule is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiple testing

anchor.snippet: When multiple true null hypothesis tests are conducted at once without adjustment

annotations.note: Multiple-testing corrections are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bootstrap hypothesis testing

anchor.snippet: A bootstrap creates numerous simulated samples by randomly resampling (with replacement) the original, combined sample data

annotations.note: Bootstrap resampling/testing is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Human sex ratio (Arbuthnot)

anchor.snippet: Arbuthnot examined birth records in London for each of the 82 years from 1629 to 1710

annotations.note: This historical sign-test example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lady tasting tea

anchor.snippet: claimed to be able to tell whether the tea or the milk was added first to a cup

annotations.note: The lady-tasting-tea example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Clairvoyant card game

anchor.snippet: A person (the subject) is tested for clairvoyance

annotations.note: The clairvoyant card-game example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neyman–Pearson lemma

anchor.snippet: The Neyman–Pearson lemma of hypothesis testing says that a good criterion for the selection of hypotheses is the ratio of their probabilities

annotations.note: The likelihood ratio (`llr`/Radon–Nikodym derivative) is formalized, but the Neyman–Pearson optimality lemma itself is not.

annotations.label: Radioactive shielded container (three hypotheses)

anchor.snippet: a test might be used to select among three hypotheses

annotations.note: This three-hypothesis example is not formalized in Mathlib.
